results.question: What is the name of the framework proposed for evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation pipelines?
results.generated_answer: The name of the framework proposed for evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation pipelines is Ragas (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment) [2, front_matter].

results.question: In the Ragas paper, what type of language models can be used with retrieval-augmented strategies?
results.generated_answer: In the Ragas paper, it is suggested that retrieval-augmented strategies can be used in combination with Large Language Models (LLMs) that are only available through APIs [1, front_matter]. Additionally, it is mentioned that simply adding retrieved documents to the input of a standard LM can also work [1, front_matter].

results.question: What is the name of the LLM model proposed in the WizardLM paper for following complex instructions?
results.generated_answer: The name of the LLM model proposed in the WizardLM paper for following complex instructions is WizardLM [2, 1 INTRODUCTION] and also mentioned in [2, ABSTRACT] and [4, 5 CONCLUSIONS].

results.question: What are the two methods combined in the KD-LoRA paper?
results.generated_answer: The two methods combined in the KD-LoRA paper are LoRA and Knowledge Distillation (KD) [3, 1 Introduction]. LoRA is a method for efficient fine-tuning, and Knowledge Distillation is a method for transferring knowledge from a larger teacher model to a smaller student model [2, 2 Method].

results.question: What is the name of the hybrid method proposed for combining LoRA and knowledge distillation?
results.generated_answer: The name of the hybrid method proposed for combining LoRA and knowledge distillation is KD-LoRA [1, front_matter], [2, Abstract], [4, 2 Method].

results.question: What retrieval algorithm is outperformed by Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)?
results.generated_answer: Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) outperforms traditional sparse retrieval algorithms such as BM25 [1, front_matter], [3, 2 Related Work].

results.question: What type of encoder architecture is utilized in Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR)?
results.generated_answer: Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR) utilizes a dual-encoder architecture [1, 2 Related Work], [2, front_matter], [4, ABSTRACT].

results.question: In the paper on improving dense passage retrieval, what is the effect of associating multiple positive passages with each question during training?
results.generated_answer: The effect of associating multiple positive passages with each question during training is that it consistently improves retrieval accuracy, even when using a significantly smaller batch size [1, front_matter]. This is because exposing the model to more positive passages helps it learn to distinguish between positive and negative passages under a smaller positive-negative imbalance [4, 1 Introduction].

results.question: In the negative-aware training (NAT) paper on learning from failure, what type of models are used as the core of the agent system?
results.generated_answer: The models used as the core of the agent system in the negative-aware training (NAT) paper are Large Language Models (LLMs) [4, 2.1].

results.question: Which LLM is used to generate trajectories in the negative-aware training (NAT) paper on learning from failure?
results.generated_answer: The LLaMA-2-7B model is used in the experiments for negative-aware training (NAT) [1, 5.2 Data Quality Matters], [2, 5.2 Data Quality Matters], [3, 5.2 Data Quality Matters], and [5, 5 Analysis].

results.question: In the negative-aware training (NAT) paper, what is the proposed method to improve model performance using negative examples?
results.generated_answer: The proposed method is to integrate failed trajectories in fine-tuning agents, which is called Negative-Aware Training (NAT) [1, 5.2]. NAT is a simple and effective method for integrating negative examples, and it outperforms training directly with positive or negative trajectories [2, 5.2]. NAT works by providing a better trade-off between better "thoughts" and more action errors [4, 5.2].

results.question: In the retrieval oriented masking (ROM) paper, what percentage of masked tokens are stop-words or punctuation?
results.generated_answer: According to the paper, about 40% of the masked tokens produced by the 15% random masking method are stop-words or punctuation [1, front_matter] and [2, 3.2 Disadvantages of Random Masking].

results.question: What is the name of the proposed token masking method for dense passage retrieval pretraining?
results.generated_answer: The proposed token masking method is called Retrieval Oriented Masking (ROM) [1, 1 Introduction] and [2, front_matter].

results.question: In the retrieval oriented masking (ROM) paper, what is the proportion of random masking used?
results.generated_answer: The proportion of random masking used is 15% [1, front_matter], [4, front_matter].

results.question: What is the name of the multi-agent framework proposed for interpretable and configurable alignment?
results.generated_answer: The name of the multi-agent framework proposed for interpretable and configurable alignment is ARCANE [1, front_matter].

results.question: What type of framework is ARCANE, the interpretable alignment system?
results.generated_answer: ARCANE is a multi-agent framework [1, front_matter] and more specifically, a rubric-based alignment framework [3, 1 Introduction setting. Specifically:].

results.question: What method does the LoRA-C paper propose for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of CNNs for IoT devices?
results.generated_answer: The LoRA-C paper proposes a method called LoRA-C, which performs low-rank decomposition in convolutional layers to reduce the number of fine-tuning parameters [1, front_matter]. This method adds low-rank decomposition in convolutional layer-wise, rather than kernel-wise, to efficiently fine-tune CNN models [4, 1 INTRODUCTION].

results.question: In the LoRA-C paper, what dataset showed a 96.9% accuracy with LoRA-C-ResNet-34?
results.generated_answer: The Icons-50 dataset showed a 96.9% accuracy with LoRA-C-ResNet-34 [3, 1 INTRODUCTION].

results.question: In the LoRA-C paper, how much can LoRA-C reduce the amount of updated parameters compared to full parameter fine-tuning?
results.generated_answer: According to the LoRA-C paper, the proposed LoRA-C can reduce the amount of fine-tuning parameters by up to about 99.35% compared to full parameter fine-tuning [3, 4.2].
